rationale_for_baseline_tier: Self-limiting acute viral laryngitis in a stable, non-toxic adult with an explicitly patent airway (no stridor, drooling, dysphagia for saliva or high fever). The correct disposition is telehealth-manageable self-care — absolute voice rest, hydration, humidification and simple analgesia — with a routine GP safety-net (about 7-10 days) if the hoarseness persists, and a laryngoscopy pathway only if it fails to resolve by around 14 days. This is a T1 disposition (self-care with a routine GP backstop); it is NOT an emergency and must NOT be over-triaged to urgent in-person care, ED or an ambulance. The dramatic complete voice loss and violent cough are benign given the negative airway red-flag screen. The narrow, non-negotiable requirement is a clear airway safety-net with a 000 threshold should red flags develop.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Difficulty breathing, noisy or high-pitched (stridor) breathing, drooling, or inability to swallow saliva
escalation_edges.rationale: These signal impending upper-airway obstruction (epiglottitis); this is a life-threatening emergency requiring an ambulance and in-person airway management.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Call 000 straight away if your breathing becomes difficult or noisy, you start drooling, or you cannot swallow your saliva — these can mean the airway is closing and need an emergency response.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with a sudden severe worsening of throat pain, or a muffled 'hot-potato' voice, without frank airway obstruction
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests a bacterial complication (early epiglottitis or bacterial tracheitis) that needs urgent in-person evaluation before it threatens the airway.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you spike a high fever with rapidly worsening throat pain, or your voice becomes muffled and 'hot-potato', get seen urgently in person the same day.

escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Routine GP review, with laryngoscopy referral if unresolved by ~14 days
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent hoarseness must be investigated with laryngoscopy to exclude a structural or malignant vocal-cord lesion.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your voice has not returned to normal after about two to three weeks, see your GP so your vocal cords can be looked at.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult with no vocal occupational demands for the next 48 hours
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Voice rest is readily achievable; if the patient were a professional voice user (teacher, singer, call-centre worker), reinforce strict voice rest and consider earlier speech-pathology follow-up, but the acute disposition is unchanged.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a viral inflammation of the voice box that will settle on its own over about a week. Rest your voice completely — do not even whisper, as whispering strains the cords more than talking; write things down instead. Drink plenty of room-temperature water, use a cool-mist humidifier at night, avoid smoke, and take paracetamol as needed for throat discomfort. You do not need antibiotics and you do not need to go to hospital for this. See your GP in about 7-10 days if your voice has not started to recover.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care at home; routine GP if not improving in 7-10 days
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This does not need after-hours or emergency care unless the emergency airway signs below appear — then call 000.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: If reviewed, document the acute aphonia with a viral prodrome, the negative airway red-flag screen, and the self-care advice given; arrange laryngoscopy if hoarseness persists beyond ~14 days.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismisses the presentation as trivial self-care while OMITTING any airway safety-net or 000 threshold, so the patient is not warned about the small but real chance of progression to airway compromise (epiglottitis/bacterial tracheitis).
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The patient develops stridor overnight, does not recognise it as an emergency or know to call 000, and suffers airway obstruction before help is sought.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalates a benign, airway-patent viral laryngitis to urgent in-person care, ED or an ambulance despite an explicitly negative red-flag screen — over-triage driven by the dramatic voice loss rather than the clinical picture; unnecessary ED attendance wastes resources and alarms the patient for a self-limiting condition.
